title: Congressional Border Security Assessment Act
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.
plain_summary: This bill lets members of Congress and their staff visit Native American reservations that are near the Mexico border. They can only visit reservations that have at least 50 miles of border with Mexico. The Congress members can use roads and paths on tribal land during these visits. The purpose is to check how well border security is working to keep the country safe.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Congressional Border Security Assessment Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Access to Indian Reservations for Border Security Assessment
sections.text: Any member of Congress and their staff can legally visit Indian reservations that have fifty miles or more of border with Mexico. They can visit these places to gather information about national security, public safety, and border security. Congress members and their staff can also use any roads or paths that cross through Indian country (land that belongs to Native American tribes as defined by federal law). The term Indian country means the same thing as defined in section eleven fifty-one of title eighteen of the United States Code.
